metadata.title: Why Banning AI from Assignments Usually Teaches the Wrong Lesson
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut for claude-for-education-a-practitioners-guide candidate 07. Source chapter: claude-for-education-a-practitioners-guide/chapters/10-student-use-from-ban-to-boundary.md (ban liabilities + four-mode framework + redesigned assignment; no equity section, no disclosure format).
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper on newsprint; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, TEAL = structured boundary / teachable mode, CRIMSON = ban failure / detection failure / undetected submission

metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = structured mode / visible boundary / teachable; CRIMSON = blanket ban / detection failure / wrong lesson; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO specific AI detection tools by name; NO equity/surveillance depth; NO Learning Mode feature; NO full disclosure format template. Core mechanism only: ban assumes the problem is access; the problem is design; four modes create teachable boundaries.

beats.narration_text: A community college instructor added 'no AI tools' to every assignment sheet. By November, her detection tool had flagged a student writing in her second language. By December, she had a policy that generated more conflict than it prevented.
beats.card_text:
No AI tools.
— on every assignment sheet
beats.notes: CARD: the rule. CRIMSON. Narration describes the November / December cascade.

beats.narration_text: At the same time, the student who genuinely struggled — the one who used Claude substantially — got through undetected. She had the wrong instinct. She had an unworkable tool.
beats.notes: STILL: community college classroom, instructor at a desk with a stack of papers, concerned expression, editorial desaturated style.

beats.narration_text: A no-AI policy should protect student cognitive labor. Here is the case where it misidentified which students were using AI and left the design problem untouched. Why?
beats.card_text:
A no-AI policy should protect
student cognitive labor.
Here it didn't. Why?
beats.notes: THE QUESTION beat — named on screen AND narration.

beats.narration_text: Banning the tool does not redesign the work. An assignment that can be completed by AI without student thinking is a design failure, not an access problem. The ban adds a rule that is difficult to enforce. It does not fix what the assignment was asking.
beats.notes: Two-box diagram: left box CRIMSON — 'AI BAN' with arrow pointing to 'DETECTION' (CRIMSON). Right side: 'assignment unchanged'. A gap arrow points down: 'design failure not addressed'. Clean, minimal.

beats.narration_text: The alternative is four modes. Prohibited — for in-class or foundational-skill work where unassisted performance is the point. Permitted with Disclosure — when AI is a plausible tool but judgment survives the assistance. Required for Process — when AI literacy is itself the outcome. Reflective Use — when metacognition is the learning goal.
beats.notes: Four TEAL blocks stacked or in a 2x2 grid: MODE 1 PROHIBITED / MODE 2 PERMITTED WITH DISCLOSURE / MODE 3 REQUIRED FOR PROCESS / MODE 4 REFLECTIVE USE. Each with a one-line note. Appear in sequence.

beats.narration_text: The instructor redesigns the research essay. Week 1: 200-word problem statement, timed in-class — no AI. Week 2: annotated source log — may use AI to locate sources but must document it. Week 3: draft with AI-use log attached. Week 4: ten-minute oral conference. Week 5: final essay with revision note. The policy is now: AI is permitted with disclosure at stages 2 and 3. The oral conference is not AI-assisted. Students can follow this. The reasoning is visible.
beats.notes: Horizontal five-stage timeline. Mode tag appears at each milestone: Mode 1 (CRIMSON dot), Mode 2 (TEAL dot), Mode 2 (TEAL), Mode 1 (CRIMSON — oral), Mode 2 (TEAL). Labels below each dot.

beats.narration_text: The real question is not: did a student use AI? It is: did a student do the thinking the assignment was designed to develop? Those are different questions with different pedagogical responses.
beats.card_text:
Did they do the thinking
the assignment was
designed to develop?
beats.notes: Quote card — the diagnostic question shift.

beats.narration_text: Three questions for each assignment. One: what intellectual skill is this assignment designed to build? Two: can AI produce the artifact without that skill being exercised? Three: which mode — prohibited, permitted with disclosure, required for process, reflective — fits this assignment's purpose? Name the mode. State the reason. Students can follow a rule they understand.
beats.notes: Three numbered question cards appear in sequence. TEAL numbering. Each question writes in. After all three: small TEAL note: 'name the mode. state the reason.'

beats.narration_text: Detection is not pedagogy. Assessment is. The ban addresses the wrong problem. The design addresses the right one.
beats.card_text:
Detection is not pedagogy.
Assessment is.
beats.notes: Recap endcard. AI IN EDUCATION topic kicker.
